coredata.dc:title: Identifying the reducing capacity of biomass derived hydrochar with different post-treatment methods
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Yan
Ma, Xianlong
Cao, Weimin
Zhang, Xiaolei
Zhou, Jizhi
Liu, Qiang
Qian, Guangren
coredata.dc:description: In this study, hydrochar was prepared from wheat straw (WS) and Spartina alterniflora (SA) biomass by hydrothermal carbonization, and further treated with HCl and NaOH washing, HNO3 oxidization and low temperature thermal heating. The reducing capacity (RC) of sample was quantified by I2 titration to explore how these modification methods affected the redox properties of hydrochar. The results indicated HNO3 and thermal oxidization increased the RC of hydrochar by 2–5 folds while NaOH washing had the negative effect on samples' RC. By analyzing the excitation-emission matrix (EEM) fluorescence of alkaline extraction solution of sample, humic acid like substances generated from various methods were identified as one of the major sources for electron donating. HNO3 oxidization could significantly increase the RC in hydrochar, which was likely resulting from the generation of alkali-soluble small molecule organic compounds. However, excessive oxidation by nitric acid with prolonged duration led to the gradual decrease in hydrochar's RC. Heating treatment caused a significant increase in the content of redox-active oxygen-containing functional groups and persistent free radicals (PFRs) in hydrochar. Even though both could donate electrons in the redox reaction with I2, the former was considered a greater contributor for the RC of hydrochar. From this study, the origin of RC of hydrochar can be identified as: oxygen-containing functionality, humic-like matter and PFRs. By employing different modification methods, the RC of hydrochar could be tuned by regulating the above sources. This study provided fundamental knowledges and simple routes to manipulate the redox properties of hydrochar for different environmental applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrochar
Reducing capacity
Electron donator
Humic acid
PFRs

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Reducing capacity of hydrochar after alkaline extraction
EEM fluorescence analysis of alkaline extraction of hydrochar
Total phenols in alkaline extraction of hydrochar

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Full-scan XPS of WSC, WSC-T and WSC-N0.5 (a); deconvolution of XPS C1s for WSC (b), WSC-T (c) and WSC-N0.5 (d).
The reducing capacity of hydrochar with different modification methods before and after alkaline washing.
EEM contours, excitation and emission loadings of four components identified by PARAFAC analysis.
The correlation between total phenol concentration and the reducing capacity of hydrochar.
EPR spectra of hydrochar with different modification methods.
(a) Cr(VI) reduction by WS hydrochar; (b) the correlation between Cr(VI) reducing amounts and the reducing capacity of WS and SA hydrochar modified by different methods.
The yield, BET-N2 surface area, reducing capacity and elemental composition of hydrochar samples (wt%, ash free).

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Obtained by assuming samples contains C, H, N, and O only.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Total phenol concentration of humic acid-like in hydrochar.
Persistent free radical content in WSC hydrochar.

row.entry:

(mmol e−·g−1)

Persistent free radical electron content, equals to spin value/N

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Ratio, equals to persistent free radical electron content/reducing capacity of hydrochar.

head.ce:title: Identifying the reducing capacity of biomass derived hydrochar with different post-treatment methods

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Shanghai University, No. 99 Shangda Road, Shanghai 200444, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Electric Power Research Institute of Yunnan Power Grid Co., Ltd, Kunming, Yunnan 650217, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Electric Power Research Institute of Yunnan Power Grid Co., Ltd

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Sciences, Shanghai University, No. 99 Shangda Rd., Shanghai 200444, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Environmental Engineering, Kyungpook National University, 80 Daehak-ro, Buk-gu 41566, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, hydrochar was prepared from wheat straw (WS) and Spartina alterniflora (SA) biomass by hydrothermal carbonization, and further treated with HCl and NaOH washing, HNO3 oxidization and low temperature thermal heating. The reducing capacity (RC) of sample was quantified by I2 titration to explore how these modification methods affected the redox properties of hydrochar. The results indicated HNO3 and thermal oxidization increased the RC of hydrochar by 2–5 folds while NaOH washing had the negative effect on samples' RC. By analyzing the excitation-emission matrix (EEM) fluorescence of alkaline extraction solution of sample, humic acid like substances generated from various methods were identified as one of the major sources for electron donating. HNO3 oxidization could significantly increase the RC in hydrochar, which was likely resulting from the generation of alkali-soluble small molecule organic compounds. However, excessive oxidation by nitric acid with prolonged duration led to the gradual decrease in hydrochar's RC. Heating treatment caused a significant increase in the content of redox-active oxygen-containing functional groups and persistent free radicals (PFRs) in hydrochar. Even though both could donate electrons in the redox reaction with I2, the former was considered a greater contributor for the RC of hydrochar. From this study, the origin of RC of hydrochar can be identified as: oxygen-containing functionality, humic-like matter and PFRs. By employing different modification methods, the RC of hydrochar could be tuned by regulating the above sources. This study provided fundamental knowledges and simple routes to manipulate the redox properties of hydrochar for different environmental applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrochar is a type of carbon-condensed material obtained from the hydrothermal carbonization of biomass/biowaste (Sevilla et al., 2011). In recent years, hydrochar receives extensive attention due to its mild preparation condition and the ability to process wet feedstock without pre-drying (Kang et al., 2012). During hydrothermal carbonization, biomass/biowaste is heated with the presence of subcritical water and autogenous pressure and undergoes hydrolysis, dehydration, decarboxylation, aromatization and re-condensation reactions (He et al., 2013). Dependent on raw materials and reaction conditions, some genotoxic substances, such as polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, phenol and phenol derivatives, are likely to be produced during hydrothermal carbonization. These toxic substances can cause negative impact on seed germination and plant growth (George et al., 2012). However, Busch et al. (2013) revealed the toxic effect of hydrochar could be eliminated by chemical or biological methods and some post-treated hydrochars even showed positive stimulating effects. Owing to the aqueous circumstance of carbonization, the surface of hydrochar possesses abundant oxygen containing functional groups (Sevilla et al., 2011), which admits hydrochar with versatile functionalities and the great potentials in heavy metals and organic compounds immobilization. For example, Fang et al. (2015b) reported hydorchar can effectively remove methylene blue and lead via complexation with surface functional groups. Therefore, hydrochar is increasingly recognized as a promising environmental remediation material.

In recent years, a lot of experiments indicated that char can act as the electron shuttle to facilitate the biodegradation of organic compounds and take part in the abiotic reduction of heavy metals (Saquing et al., 2016; Xu et al., 2015). For example, the chemical transformation from Cr(VI) to Cr(III) was detected in Cr(VI) sorption by char, which was considered to be regulated by the phenolic moieties in carbonaceous materials (Dong et al., 2011; Shen et al., 2012). Kluepfel et al. (2014) characterized the redox properties of biochar and revealed that redox-active moieties are electron-donating phenolic groups for low temperature char, electron accepting quinone groups for intermediate temperature char, and quinones and condensed aromatics for high temperature char. However, to our best knowledge, little information is available on the redox property of hydrochar. In a recent work, Chen et al. (2017) reported hydrochar owns photochemical activity to generate reactive oxygen species for the degradation of organic contaminant which are associated with the surface redox active sites. Therefore, the elaborate application for pollution remediation requires sound knowledge on the redox property of hydrochars.

Hydrochar is different greatly from biochar in chemical properties. Published literatures revealed hydrothermal carbonization usually resulted in low degree carbonized char that mainly contained alkyl moieties with much less aromaticity (Cao et al., 2010). Therefore, the redox active conjugated π-electron system associated with condensed aromatic clusters can be ruled out from hydrochar. Our previous studies specified that hydrochar presented much more reducing capacity (RC) than oxidizing capacity (OC), and the RC was associated with the reductive moieties of hydrochar such as phenolic groups (Mai et al., 2017; Xu et al., 2014). This confirmed the surface functionality is one of the major source of electron donating in hydrochar. In addition, the characterization of char-derived dissolved organic matter (DOM) proved that humic-like substances are abundant in char, especially for those obtained under low temperature (Jamieson et al., 2014; Zhang et al., 2014). It is well known that humic-like substances are rich in reversible redox sites that can participate in a lot of redox transformation of organic and inorganic species in environment (Ratasuk and Nanny, 2007; Palmer and von Wandruszka, 2010). Therefore, DOM is supposed to make an important contribution to the redox property of hydrochar. In addition, recent studies reported that the carbonization process could produce a large number of persistent free radicals (PFRs) in char (Fang et al., 2014; Fang et al., 2015a; Liao et al., 2014). The content of free radicals was reported to have a direct relationship with the reduction of Fe(III) by natural organic matter (Palmer and von Wandruszka, 2010). Therefore, PFR is probably a candidate pool to donate electrons in hydrochar.

As such, the major objectives of this study were: 1) to identify the source of RC on hydrochar; 2) to elucidate how post-treatment affects the RC of samples. In this experiment, we prepared the hydrochars from two common biomass wastes and post-treated these samples by acid/base treatment, HNO3 oxidization and heating treatment. These post-methods were selected because they are frequently used to modify the property (especially for surface functionality) of biochar/hydrochar for particular purposes. For example, HCl treatment was reported to increase the specific surface area (Ryu et al., 2015) and reduce surface negative charge (Dong et al., 2017) of char. NaOH treatment leads to the increase of lactone but the decrease of phenol, while HNO3 treatment makes more carboxyl acid groups developed on char (Li et al., 2014). Our previous study revealed heating treatment can significantly improve the RC of hydrochar. The hydrochar samples from different post-treatment methods were further employed to reduce Cr(VI) to verify their environmental application.

The biomass of wheat straw (WS) and Spartina alterniflora (SA) were used for the preparation of hydochar, respectively. Wheat straw is generated in a huge amount annually around the world. SA is an invasive herb widely distributed in the tidal stretch of coastal region in East and South China seas. Both WS and SA biomass were harvested from Chongming Island, shanghai, China. To prepare raw hydrochar, 10.0 g biomass powder (<100 mesh) were thoroughly mixed with 50 ml deionized water and loaded in a 100-ml poly(tetrafluoroethylene)-lined stainless steel autoclave. N2 (99.99%, 100 ml·min−1) was used to purge the mixture for 30 min to get rid of dissolved oxygen in solution. Hydrothermal carbonization was performed in an oven at 240 °C for 4 h. The pressure (3.4 MPa) in the hydrothermal processes was self-generated by closed chamber reactors. The solid sample was withdrawn by collecting the pellets after centrifuging (3000 rpm for 10 min) the autoclaved sample. Hydrochar powder was alternately rinsed with acetone and deionized water until the pH reached stable. The resulted sample was dried using an oven at 105 °C for 24 h, ground to pass through a 100-mesh sieve and stored under N2 for experimental use. Samples derived from WS and SA biomass were marked with WSC and SAC, respectively.

HNO3 oxidation of hydrochar was performed by soaking WSC or SAC (10.0 g) into 30% HNO3 solution (10 ml) at boiling temperature (about 70 °C). After a predefined period, the oxidized sample was withdrawn and rinsed with deionized water until a constant pH was reached. After that, the sample was dried at 50 °C in a vacuum chamber overnight and stored under N2. HNO3 oxidized sample was labeled as WSC/SAC-NX, where X represents the oxidation time (i.e., 0.5, 2, 4 or 8 h).

Acid/base treatment of hydrochar was carried out by soaking raw hydrochar (1.0 g) in 6 M HCl or NaOH (100 ml) for 24 h, followed by the same rinsing procedure as the HNO3 oxidization treatment. Acid/base-treated samples were labeled as WSC/SAC-H and WSC/SAC-OH, respectively.

To prepare the thermal treated sample, 1.0 g hydrochar was heated by an oven in air at 240 °C for 10 min. After being cooled down to room temperature, sample was ground to pass through a 100-mesh sieve for experimental use. Thermal treated hydrochar was marked as WSC/SAC-T.

Hydrochar's RC was quantified by a titration method, where 0.05 M iodine solution (I2) was used as titrant (Palmer and von Wandruszka, 2010; Struyk and Sposito, 2001). Titration vessel was a 120-ml polystyrene container with a screw-top cap modified to accommodate a redox electrode (ORP501, Shanghai Leici), a gas inlet tube, and a 0.1-ml microburet. Redox potential was recorded with a redox potential meter (ZDJ-5, Shanghai Leici). Titration curve was obtained by plotting the redox potential of titrating solution against I2 amount (an example is given in Fig. S1). Before titration, 0.1 g hydrochar were dispersed homogeneously in 100 ml phosphate buffer (pH 7.01). The suspension was bubbled with N2 (99.99%, 100 ml·min−1) for at least 2 h to stabilize the solution pH and redox potential. Once the variation in redox potential was <2.0 mV·min−1 (Fig. S1, point A), the electrode was lifted and 0.05 ml I2 (0.05 M) was titrated into the solution. To facilitate I2 contact with the hydrochar, the gas inlet tube was introduced into the solution to flow through 100 ml·min−1 N2 gas for 2 min. After that, the electrode was immersed into the supernatant again for the record of redox potential (Fig. S1, point B). Titration continued virtually until a constant redox potential was achieved after I2 addition (Fig. S1, point C). For a single sample, a full titration process usually required about 5–10 h. The RC of hydrochar was obtained from the total volume of I2 consumed at equilibrium (Point C, Fig. S1) by assuming the following redox reactions proceeded.
(1)
I 2
+
2e −
=
2I −
(2)
Biochar
red
−
2e −
=
Biochar
ox
According to the conservation of electron gain and loss in a reaction, the RC of hydrochar can be determined by the amounts of electron donated by iodine.

Ten milligrams of the hydrochar were loaded into a nuclear magnetic tube and continuously monitored for free radical signals on an electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectrometer (Bruker Elexsys 580) with a single cavity, a modulation of 100 kHz and microwave frequencies of 9.2–9.9 GHz. The parameters for EPR detection were as follow: the sweep width was 100 G, the modulation amplitude was 1.00 G and the resolution in the X axes was 1024 points. EPR microwave power was set specifically to 31 dB (or 0.131 mW) and the sweep time was 20.48 ms. DPPH (2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl) was used as a standard to quantify the content of PFRs. The g factor of each signal was obtained from EPR spectrogram.

The abundance of elemental C, N, and H in hydrochar were determined by an Elemental Analyzer (EA3000 EuroVector EURO), and oxygen content was calculated by assuming that hydrochar was composed of C, N, H, and O only (Fang et al., 2015a). BET surface area was measured by a Micromeritics ASAP 2020 using N2 as adsorbate at −195.74 °C. XPS (ESCALAB 250 Xi, USA) was used to determine elemental composition and chemical state of elements on hydrochar surface. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) core level spectra were analyzed using XPS Peak 4.1 software. The surface oxygen functional groups in hydrochar were quantified by temperature-programmed CO and CO2 desorption (TPD) analysis according to our previous study (Mai et al., 2017). The TPD test was carried out under a N2 flow of 50 ml · min−1 and a heating rate of 3 °C · min up to 800 °C. Effluent CO and CO2 were monitored by a gas chromatograph (GC7900, Techcomp Ltd., China) equipped with FID and μ-TCD detector.

Liquid sample is the alkaline extraction solution of hydrochar, which was prepared by shaking 0.1 g hydrochar with 100 ml NaOH solution (0.05 M) in 100 ml Schott reagent bottle for 24 h, followed by filtration via 0.22 μm membrane filter. Liquid sample was airtightly stored at 4 °C and analyzed within 24 h.

Total phenol concentration in liquid was determined by Folin-Ciocalteu (FC) assay. FC reaction involves the oxidation of phenols and other compounds by a phosphomolybdic tungstic acid reagent in basic medium (pH 10) (Pradhan and Sandle, 1999). Gallic acid was used to develop a standard curve, and the concentration of total phenols was reported in gallic acid equivalents (GAE) as μmol GAE·g−1. In a typical run, 0.5 ml liquid sample and 0.5 ml FC reagent were mixed in a 10 ml colorimetric tube to develop color for 2 min, followed by the addition of 1 ml Na2CO3 (20%) and 8 ml deionized water. The absorbance of the solution was determined by FC assay at 725 nm on an ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometer (752 N, China). The total phenol concentration was calculated by the standard curve established previously. All chemicals and reagents (Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., China) used in this study were in AR-grade.

Liquid sample was diluted by 20 times with deionized water to ensure adequate transmittance. The excitation-emission matrix (EEM) fluorescence of sample was recorded by a F97PRO spectrofluorometer (Shanghai lengguang technology company, Shanghai, China) using a 1 cm quartz cuvette. The scanning field was set at excitation from 200 to 600 nm and emission from 200 to 700 nm with 5 and 1 nm sampling interval on excitation (Ex) and emission (Em) modes, respectively. All fluorescence data were presented in arbitrary units. Parallel factor analysis (PARAFAC) was conducted using DOMFluor toolbox (Stedmon and Bro, 2008), which contains all of the tools used to identify the outlier samples as well as to perform split-half analysis and residual error diagnosis. The outlier occurred when a sample contained some measurement error or it was properly measured but differed greatly from the others (Stedmon and Bro, 2008). After preliminary steps (i.e., data loading, scattering removal, initial explorative data analysis, and outlier identification), PARAFAC models with 2 to 7 components were computed. Residual analysis, visual inspection and split half analysis were performed to determine the correct numbers of components.

Cr(VI) stock solution (100 mg·l−1) was prepared by dissolving 0.2829 g K2Cr2O7 (AR) into 1000 ml deionized water with pH adjusted by HCl to about 2.0. Cr(VI) reduction by hydrochar was conducted by batch equilibrium test. In typical, 0.16 g sample were dispersed into 160 mL Cr(VI) stock solution and agitated in a temperature-controlled shaker (25 °C) at 180 rpm. After a defined interval, liquid sample was withdrawn and filtered for the measurement of Cr. The concentration of Cr(VI) was determined on a UV spectrophotometer (752 N, Shanghai Jinke, China) and the concentration of total chromium was measured with an inductive coupled plasma emission spectrometer (ICP-AES, Prodidy, Leeman Co., USA).

Table 1 shows the results of yield, elemental analysis and BET-N2 measurement of hydrochar. As observed, the modification of hydrochar by different methods led to an obvious change in the yield of sample. In general, the hydrochar treated with HCl gave the highest productivities (i.e., 97.4% for WSC and 98.2% for SAC). The slight loss of yield was likely attributed to the dissolution of inorganic alkaline components (such as carbonate, oxide and hydroxide, etc.) in acid washing (Tsechansky and Graber, 2014). NaOH treatment caused a loss of about 30% in the mass of hydrochar. This could be the reason that hydrochar was abundant with organic acids and phenolic compounds (Tsechansky and Graber, 2014; Zhang et al., 2014). Part of these organic matters could be eliminated by repeated water/ethanol washing steps, while some residues could still be adsorbed onto the surface or inside the pores of hydrochar. When contacted with NaOH, these substances could be dissolved out (Tsechansky and Graber, 2014). The great difference between the yields of sample before and after NaOH washing suggested organic acid accounted for a large proportion in hydrochar. Of these modification methods, thermal treatment resulted in the lowest yield (i.e., 36.1% for WSC-H and 30.2% for SAC-H). It was because that the carbon fragments and DOM (Riedel et al., 2015; Zhu et al., 2015) adhered on the hydrochar surface and parts of the outer layer carbon atoms at the edge of aromatization structure were vigorously oxidized under air (Guo et al., 2015). For HNO3 oxidization, the yield of sample dramatically decreased with increasing treatment time (e.g., the yield for WSC decreased from 93.8% to 32.5% with the oxidizing duration being prolonged from 0.5 to 8 h). According to one previous study, long period of HNO3 oxidation could lead to the break of carbon skeleton and the product of some low-molecular soluble organic compounds (Jin et al., 2013). This could explain why the productivity of hydrochar reduced significantly with the increasing HNO3 oxidization duration.

BET characterization showed that the surface areas of two raw hydrochars were 15.99 and 13.36 m2·g−1 for WSC and SAC, respectively (Table 1). However, the specific surface area of hydrochar significantly increased (up to 157.69 m2·g−1, about 10 times higher than raw hydrochar) after thermal treatment. The high surface area was likely attributed to the volatilization or oxidization of the small molecular organic compounds covering or blocking the pores of hydrochar (Ahmad et al., 2013; Li et al., 2014). Acid treatment showed insignificant impact on the specific surface area of samples, which was consistent with the variation of the yield of hydrochar after HCl washing. Interestingly, the alkaline treatment and HNO3 oxidation slightly reduced the surface area of sample. This could be ascribed to that some of the small molecular humics dissolved by NaOH further entered into the micropores of hydrochar during the alkaline treatment (Fan et al., 2010). With respect to HNO3 oxidization, previous studies suggested this modification method could lead to the collapse of pores and expansion of micro-pores to meso- or macro-pores in carbonaceous materials (Ania et al., 2002; Villacañas et al., 2006). In addition, the development of oxygen containing functional groups inside the pores of hydrochar led by HNO3 oxidation could prevent the access of nitrogen molecules during BET analysis (López-Garzón et al., 2003), which resulted in the decrease of surface area.

As presented by Table 1, HCl and NaOH treatments had very little impact on the final composition of hydrochar, which agreed well with the previous studies (Fan et al., 2010; Li et al., 2014). Thermal treatment caused an increase in the oxygen content of hydrochar, which was attributed to the development of oxygen containing functional groups (such as carboxyl and carbonyl groups) during the oxidation (Xu et al., 2014). Elemental analysis results showed that the C content decreased, while the O content increased in the samples after HNO3 oxidation. The decreased C content also suggested some of the soluble organic matter were generated after HNO3 oxidation. The increased O content proved the development of oxygen containing functionality on hydrochar as a result of chemical oxidation (Kaper et al., 2008; Sasmal et al., 2012). In addition, an increase in N content was detected in the HNO3 oxidized samples. This was likely resulting from the formation of some nitro groups or the irreversible adsorption of nitrate ions on hydrochar (Pradhan and Sandle, 1999; Stavropoulos et al., 2008).

Molar H/C ratio is usually employed to represent the degree of aromaticity in carbon-based materials. As shown in Table 1, HNO3 oxidation significantly increased the H/C ratio in hydrochar, which suggested an increase in organic components. For thermal heated treatment under air, the amount of organic residues was largely reduced. Molar O/C ratio of hydrochar can partially reflect the hydrophilicity of char surface (Chun et al., 2004). HNO3 oxidation and thermal treatment enhance the molar O/C ratio of hydrochar suggesting the surface of hydrochar became more hydrophilic and more polar groups were formed. Besides, the molar O/C ratio of WSC-N was significantly built up with the increasing processing time, which indicated longer HNO3 oxidation time produced more polar oxygen contained groups. Enhanced hydrophilicity of hydrochar surface could lead to a better contact between the chars and facilitate their interaction with the oxidant (target contaminant). The same phenomena and explanations can also be applied for the tests with SA hydrochar.

The RC of hydrochar from different modification methods are shown in Table 1. Both raw WS and SA hydrochar had an electronic donating capacity of 0.40 mmol·g−1. HCl treatment slightly reduced the RCs of WSC and SAC, while NaOH treatment led to a substantial decrease in the RCs of samples in contrast to raw hydrochar. From Table 1, thermal treatment and HNO3 oxidation caused a notable increase in RC of hydrochar. For example, the electronic donating capacity of SAC almost tripled (1.15 mmol·g−1) after the thermal oxidization in comparison with its precursor (0.40 mmol·g−1), whereas, the short-term (0.5 h) HNO3 oxidation increased the RC by four times (2.00 mmol·g−1). Surprisingly, the increasing HNO3 treatment time led to a gradual decline in the RC of sample (e.g., the RC decreased from 2.10 to 1.00 mmol·g−1 when the oxidizing time was prolonged from 0.5 to 8 h for WSC-N).

To identify the possible reasons for above findings, XPS wide-scan was employed. As shown in Fig. 1a, three major peaks of C (C1s, 292–280 eV), O (O1s, 538–528 eV) and N (N1 s, 412–394 eV) were detected in the spectra of WSC, WSC-T and WSC-N0.5. The deconvolution C1s spectra of the above samples are shown in Fig. 1(b–d) and the other samples (WSC-N2, WSC-N4 and WSC-N8) are compiled in Fig. S3. C1s main peak at 284.6 eV (peak I) is associated with the aliphatic/aromatic carbon (CH, CC, and CC) groups. The peaks at 286.2 eV (peak II), 287.5 (peak III) and 289.2 eV (peak IV) are related to the oxygen-contained moieties, (i.e., CO, CO, and OCO) (Fang et al., 2013). O1s spectra (Fig. S4) had four peaks with binding energies of 531.1, 532.3, 533.3 and 534.2 eV, which can be related to CO (peak A), CO (esters, anhydrides)/O(ethers, phenols) (peak B), O (esters, anhydrides) (peak C) and O=C–OH(peak D), respectively (Okpalugo et al., 2005). N 1s spectra (Fig. S5) showed the information regarding to the following binding energies: pyridinic (398.99 eV, peak a), pyrrolic and pyridonic (400.3 eV, peak b), NO3 (404.8 eV, peak c) and NO2 (406.6 eV, peak d) (Pietrzak, 2009).

After thermal treatment, the peak IV in C1S and peaks A, C and D in O1S of WSC-H was strengthened (Tables S2 and S3), which suggested a number of carbonyl, ester, lactone and carboxyl groups were produced. This was consistent with our previous study (Xu et al., 2014) and the other published report (Chen et al., 2011). For WSC-N samples, the peak II, III and IV in C1s and peak C and D in O1s (Tables S2 and S3) were intensified, suggesting oxygen containing functionality was also developed during HNO3 oxidation. Apart from these, N1 s spectra of these samples were significantly altered, where the intensity of peak a (pyridinic) and peak b (pyrrolic and pyridonic) was enhanced. Meanwhile, the peak c and d (–N-Ox) emerged on the spectra, which was probably due to the chemisorption of HNO3 (Hu et al., 2013; Zhu et al., 2014). Among the above nitrogen containing functional groups, only pyridinic group (peak a) was reported to have the ability to donate electron (Hu et al., 2013). Therefore, the increase in the RC of thermal treated hydrochar was mainly contributed by the oxygen-containing functional groups, more specifically, carbonyl and carboxyl groups (Table S3). For HNO3 oxidized samples, according to Table S3 and Fig. S5, the enhancement of RC should be attributed both oxygen (carboxyl groups) and nitrogen functionality (pyridinic groups).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reducing capacity of hydrochar after alkaline extraction
ce:section.ce:para: The raw, thermal treated and HNO3 oxidized hydrochar were thoroughly rinsed with alkaline solution. The solid samples were recovered and further subjected to I2 titration to quantify their RCs. Fig. 2 shows the RCs of these samples before and after alkaline washing. As observed, the RCs of WSC and SAC decreased by about 62.5% after alkaline washing, suggesting humic matter played an important role in the electronic donating capacity of hydrochar. The RCs of thermal treated samples increased (by 9.5% for WSC and 23.0% for SAC) after alkaline washing. As stated above, most of the humic matter adhered on hydrochar could be eliminated by thermal treatment under air at 240 °C. Therefore, the increase in the RCs of WSC-T and SAC-T was likely attributed to the exposure of more redox active moieties after alkaline washing. From Fig. 2, the RCs of alkaline washed WSC-N and SAC-N series significantly decreased, where these values could not be detected at samples with HNO3 oxidization duration >2 h. According to the mass loss of the recovered solid sample of hydrochar after alkaline washing (Table S1), almost all solid sample lost after alkaline extraction once HNO3 oxidation exceeded 2 h. Therefore, it can be concluded that the electron donating capacity of these samples were mainly contributed by the alkali soluble product derived from the HNO3 oxidization of hydrochar.

ce:section.ce:section-title: EEM fluorescence analysis of alkaline extraction of hydrochar
ce:section.ce:para: The EEM spectra of WSC-derived alkaline extraction solution from different post-treatment methods can be found in Fig. S6. Four major peaks were detected in these spectra, which were at Ex350/Em450, Ex280, 420/Em510, Ex330, 480/Em600 and Ex290/Em400 nm. Through the residual analysis and split half analysis, four fluorescent components were identified by the EEM-PARAFAC modeling, as shown in Fig. 3 .

Component 1 (C1) contains 350/450 peak and is often found in river/ocean water samples as humic moiety with a peak at 339–420/434–520 nm (Uchimiya et al., 2015). Component 2 (C2) showed a primary peak at Ex420 nm/Em510 nm and a secondary peak at Ex280 nm/Em510 nm, which was likely associated with the humic-like organic matter in the surface or natural water (Chen et al., 2003; Kowalczuk et al., 2009). Component 3 (C3) showed a primary peak at Ex480 nm/Em600 nm and a secondary Ex330 nm/Em600 nm, which was also a type of humic-like component found in natural water system (Chen et al., 2003; Kowalczuk et al., 2009). Component 4 (C4) (290/400) was likely related to the UVA (315–400 nm) absorbing marine humic-like moieties (Ishii and Boyer, 2012). All the components had a longer emission wavelength (Em > 380 nm), which suggested the organic matter in the alkaline extraction solution of hydrochar contained highly conjugated and aromatic, humic-like structures. Similar components (C1, C2, C4) were also observed in biochar-derived dissolved organic matter (Jamieson et al., 2014).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Total phenols in alkaline extraction of hydrochar
ce:section.ce:para: The redox-activity of humic-like substance is primarily associated with the phenolic contained components, which account for about 20–30 wt% of humic and fulvic acids (Warner et al., 2000). Therefore, total phenols in the alkaline extraction solution of hydrochar were measured. As shown in Table 2 , raw WSC and SAC had total phenol concentration of 0.14 ± 0.02 and 0.08 ± 0.01 mmol GAE g−1, respectively. The concentration of total phenol in WS/SAC-T was under the detection limit. WS/SAC-N samples showed a gradual decreased total phenol concentration with increasing oxidization time. The contents of total phenols in these samples were further correlated with the RCs of hydrochar, and the result suggested that the total phenol and RCs had a satisfied linear relationship (R 2 = 0.903) (Fig. 4 ). Therefore, the phenolic moieties were identified as an important source for the electron donating capacity of hydrochar.

Recent work indicated a mass of persistent free radicals with unpaired electrons could be formed in biochar during the pyrolysis process (Fang et al., 2014; Fang et al., 2015a). PFRs with unpaired electrons could provide electrons to participate in redox reaction. Therefore, the PFRs in WSC-T before/after I2 titration and WSC-N series were examined.

As shown in Fig. 5 , a broad singlet signal can be observed in the EPR spectra of raw WSC. Thermal treatment of WSC greatly enhanced the EPR signal intensity. However, this signal can hardly be detected in the heating treated WSC after the reaction with I2 (marked with WSC-T″). For WSC-N samples, only weak EPR signal could be monitored. The g-factors of all samples were in the range of 2.0030–2.0040. It was reported that the g-factors <2.0030 belong to carbon-centered radicals, those in the range of 2.0030–2.0040 represent for carbon-centered radicals with an adjacent oxygen atom, while oxygen-centered radicals have a g-factor in 2.0040–2.0045. Besides, the semi-quinone radicals have a g-factor larger than 2.0045 (Vejerano et al., 2010). Therefore, the PFRs detected in this experiment were the typical carbon-centered PFR with an adjacent oxygen atom free radical (Fang et al., 2015a).

By using DPPH as a reference, PFR content in hydrochar can be determined and the results are shown in Table 3 . PFRs increased significantly from 19.13 × 1017 to 65.15 × 1017 spins·g−1 for WSC-T, while decreased markedly to 1.018 × 1017 spins·g−1 after reacting with I2 (WSC-T"). The dramatic difference before and after titration suggests that those unpaired electrons in PFRs participated likely in the redox reaction with I2 and contributed to the RCs of hydrochar. PFR contents in HNO3 oxidized hydrochar were low (i.e., 1.863 × 1017, 1.794 × 1017, 0.8712 × 1017 and 3.251 × 1017 spins·g−1 for WSC-N0.5, WSC-N2, WSC-N4 and WSC-N8, respectively) and showed insignificant relevance to oxidizing duration. These results suggested the thermal treatment could substantially increase the concentration of PFRs in hydrochar, while HNO3 oxidation could not. The number of electrons supplied by PFRs can be quantified by the ratio of PFRs concentration and Avogadro's constant. As seen in Table 3, the electrons donated by the PFRs accounted for 1.03% of the RC of hydrochar at the maximum. This result revealed the contribution of PFRs to the electron donating capacity of hydrochar was very limited.

To develop possible application of hydrochar's reducing ability, Cr(VI) reduction test was carried out both for WS and SA hydrochar. Fig. 6a shows the Cr(VI) and Cr(III) concentration profiles in reduction test by WS hydrochar, and the results by SA hydrochar are compiled in Fig. S8. Within 240 min, Cr(VI) was reduced by 13.8 ± 1.2%, 47.3 ± 2.4%, 27.6 ± 0.3% and 11.1 ± 1.1% by WSC, WSC-N0.5, WSC-T and WSC-OH, respectively. Similar results were also observed for SC hydrochar. As seen from Fig. 6b, the reducing amounts of Cr(VI) showed a good linear relationship (R 2 = 0.93) with the RCs of hydrochar (including both WS and SC series). In addition, it is interesting to find that Cr(III) concentration profiles varied greatly with post-treatment methods, which reveals hyrochar had different binding ability to Cr(III) after modification. According to mass balance, WSC-N0.5 was found to show little affinity to Cr(III) because more that 80% of Cr3+ ions produced from reduction reaction were detected in liquid phase. On the contrary, WSC-T had strong affinity to Cr(III) since little Cr3+ was measured in solution. Above results indicate post-treatment also changed the functional groups on hydrochar responsible for the complexation with heavy metals.

This study suggested that the oxygen contained functionality and humic-like matters generated from hydrothermal carbonization of biomass are the major sources of hydrochar RC. Even though the condensed aromatic structure is an important electron denoting pool in biochar from pyrolysis (Kluepfel et al., 2014), we strongly believed it contributed very little to the RC of hydrochar because the graphited structure in hydrochar is usually poorly developed (Mai et al., 2017).

From this experiment, HNO3 oxidation and thermal treatment could significantly enhance the RC of hydrochar, which highlighted the feasibility to manipulate the RC of hydrochar for the remediation of environmental pollution. Our previous study demonstrated that thermal treated hydrochar can activate H2O2 to achieve an effective degradation of organic contaminant (Xu et al., 2014). The high-activity of hydrochar was supposed to be related to the increase of hydrochar RC after thermal treatment. In addition, since DOMs are able to take part in the abiotic reduction of metals and organic contaminants (Palmer and von Wandruszka, 2010; Ratasuk and Nanny, 2007; Struyk and Sposito, 2001), HNO3 oxidized hydrochar can act as a potential engineering material for environmental remediation.

According to our results, FPRs contributed less to the overall RC of hydrochar. However, it must be noted that FPRs usually exhibits a high reactivity towards the degradation of organic compounds (Chen et al., 2017). For example, Fang et al. (2014) found that co-thermal treatment of metal with phenolic compound can not only increase the density of PFRs in hydrochar but also change the types of PFRs, which provides the potential of tailoring the PFRs content for improving both the RC and reactivity of hydrochar.

Finally, even though the post-treatment methods employed in this study can regulate the redox property of hydrochar, one must be noted that to what extent these methods can reduce hydrochar genotoxic and phytotoxic risk is still open.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for the financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (415272349) and Science and Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality (No. 15230500300). We also greatly appreciate the help from the Instrumental Analysis & Research Center of Shanghai University for sample characterization.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2018.06.232.
